text: The surgical team prepares for a laparoscopic myomectomy on a patient with an 18-centimeter intramural myoma located in the fundal and posterior wall of the uterus. Four laparoscopic ports are positioned: one approximately 10 centimeters above the umbilicus, one at Palmer's point, one in the left lumbar region, and one contralateral on the right side.

The surgeon injects approximately 200 ml of diluted Vasopressin into the uterine tissue using an aspiration needle and XXC syringe, facilitating transient ischemia and hydro dissection to create a clear surgical plane.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut through the muscle and serous layers of the uterus, which appears marble white due to the vasopressin injection. The incision is enlarged to create a good plane for suction irrigation and to facilitate the use of a myoma screw to hold the myoma.

The surgeon makes a ten-centimeter incision in the uterus to accommodate the large myoma. A myoma screw is introduced through the supraumbilical port to provide traction, preventing the uterus from touching the telescope. The incision is further enlarged, and suction irrigation is used for nucleation, avoiding the risk of instrument breakage associated with harmonic or Maryland graspers.

The surgeon uses a single jaw traumatic grasper on the left side and suction irrigation on the right hand to enucleate the myoma. Blunt dissection is performed to separate the muscle layer from the myoma, ensuring the uterine cavity remains closed and the endometrium is preserved. The myoma is gradually being extracted.

The surgeon relocates the myoma screw multiple times to maintain traction during dissection. The soft myoma is gradually being extracted, though its softness complicates achieving a clear dissection plane.

The surgeon carefully completes the dissection and removes the entire myoma from the uterus. The deep intramural myoma presents a challenge for uterine repair, requiring multiple layers of suturing to prevent rupture in future pregnancies.

The surgeon completes the separation of the myoma from the uterine muscle using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the myoma is fully detached. The myoma is now removed from the surgical field. The surgeon begins suturing the inner muscle layer with a Dundee jamming knot, which serves as a starter knot for continuous suturing, securely locking the tissue.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing in the deep muscle layer of the uterus following myoma removal, using a bi-directional barb suture like V-lock to secure the sutures and prevent loosening.

The surgeon performs interlocking continuous suturing on the deeper muscle layer of the uterus, followed by interrupted extracorporeal square knots to reduce the gap. A final continuous suturing layer is applied to close the serous layer, ensuring secure three-layer closure. The suturing technique involves locking each stitch to maintain tension and prevent uterine rupture in future pregnancies. The needle remains active for traction as the surgeon begins another continuous suturing with a Dundee jamming knot.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing from below upwards on the inner muscle layer using a dandy jamming knot, ensuring the layer is securely approximated. This is part of a three-layer repair in the myomectomy procedure, focusing on the innermost layer with Aberdeen termination.

The surgeon applies a surgeon's knot on both ends of the needle to terminate the suture, completing the closure of the inner muscle layer. The suturing technique reinforces the muscle and reduces bleeding, using No.-one Vicryl thread.

The surgeon ties and locks an extracorporeal Mishra's knot as a tension suture to approximate the edges of the surgical site, visibly reducing the large gap created by the removal of the 18-centimeter myoma.

The surgeon uses a darker knot pusher to tighten and lock the extracorporeal knot, ensuring it remains secure without slipping. The knot configuration includes one hitch, three winds, and three locks, effectively obliterating dead space and reinforcing the edges of the surgical site. The tension suture minimizes dead space and prevents seroma formation, visibly approximating the edges.

The surgeon uses a blanket suture technique with extracorporeal knots, including a mattress suture, to approximate the tissue. The suture is locked multiple times for secure closure. A Melgers knot or extracorporeal square knot with a Clark knot pusher may be used, but Mishra's knot is preferred for its sliding and locking capabilities.

The surgeon uses an endoske needle with a 30 mm round body needle to take a deep bite, approximating the edges and reducing the gap left by the myoma removal. The extracorporeal knots are applied and locked to prevent slipping, ensuring the tension is maintained and the gap is closed.

The surgeon demonstrates the completion of bleeding control and edge approximation, followed by the initiation of a third layer of continuous suturing to approximate the serosa. A Dandy jamming knot is used for self-locking, allowing tight interlocking sutures. An assistant maintains traction while the surgeon continues suturing, ensuring the serosa is tightly approximated.

The surgeon applies a continuous blanket suture to tightly approximate the serous layer during the myomectomy procedure. The assistant holds the suture as the surgeon continues to take additional bites, ensuring the suture remains taut and secure.

The surgeon completes the termination of discontinuous suturing using a surgeon's knot, ensuring no bleeding and a round shape of the uterus. The procedure transitions to morcellation of the myoma using a Gynecare morcellator, with visible precautions taken to ensure safe tissue removal.
